content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies where one actor plays two/multiple roles?

**Score:** 80
**Body:** Gotta be one of my favorite tropes. Adaptation, Sinners, Dead Ringers, Dr. Strangelove, The Monkey, Alien Covenant, Infinity Pool, Parent Trap, etc. Thank you!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Watch a bunch of stuff from Eddie Murphy and Mike Myers.

Austin powers series

The nutty professor

Norbit
  - Coming to America , especially with Eddie as the old Jew in the barber shop!
2. i would say the GOAT is Tatiana Maslany in Orphan Black credited as playing...

Sarah Manning • Cosima Niehaus • Alison Hendrix • Helena • Rachel Duncan • Beth Childs • Krystal Goderitch • MK • Jennifer Fitzsimmons • Katja Obinger • Camilla Torres • Leda Clone in Church • Tony Zwickey
3. Face Off
4. Back to the future 2 and 3
5. Multiplicity. Michael Keatons amazing
6. *Cloud Atlas*, for better or worse.

Also, a less-obvious one: *The Two Towers*.  John Rhys-Davies is both Gimli and Treebeard.
7. Alex Garland’s Men.
8. Jack & Jill \[2011\] NOT a recommendation to watch it BTW!
9. Coming to America. 

## 2. I like bad movies but I'm running out.

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Me and my wife like to watch bad movies on our days off cause they are goofy and can be a lot of fun. I need movie suggestions for bad movies that are worth watching. Here's a list of a few we've already watched to help avoid things we've seen.

The room

Velosipaster

The killer piñata

Trolls 2

The killer tomatoes 

Bad cgi shark

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Oh, oh my goodness. Let me introduce you to a little channel called RedLetterMedia. They *specialize* in bad movies. For the last decade and a half on their YouTube show Best of the Worst, they've been watching 3 bad movies per episode. There is an OCEAN of bad movies waiting for you. You can use their episode list, or watch the RLM guys distill each one down to the best moments.

https://letterboxd.com/shaynepc/list/best-of-the-worst-a-list-of-all-movies-featured/detail/

Classics include Shakma, American Rickshaw, the Miami Connection, Deadly Prey, Blood Debts, Mystics in Bali, Geteven, Bigfoot vs DB Cooper, Partners, Hologram Man, Hawk Jones, Twin Dragon Encounter, Robot in the Family, and the mad man that is Neil Breen

Occasional guest hosts include Macaulay Culkin and Jack Quaid.

Wheel of the Worst episodes have them watching non-narrative films such as how-tos, infomercials, exercise videos, etc. These are torturous. However, their efforts have found some incredible diamonds: Wheel of the Worst #16 found *Surviving Edged Weapons*, a 1980s police training video with enough crazy to top a Michael Bay movie, and WotW #18 discovered *Creating Rem Lezar*, which cannot be summed up.
  - This...This.. . A THOUSAND TIMES THIS!!!!
2. Any shark movie that's not the original jaws. The meg is so bad it's good both parts. 
3. Trolls 2 😂I can’t even imagine. 

Beloved (1998)
4. Killer Klowns from Outer Space 
5. Blood Diner (1987)  
Chopping Mall (1986)  
The Carrier (1988)  
Burial Ground (1981)  
Zombie Lake (1981)  
Rats: Night of Terror (1984)  
Hell Comes to Frogtown (1988)
6. Stroll through MST3K on YT and you will get plenty of material. 
7. Showgirls 


8. Sharknado

8-legged Freaks

Big Trouble in Little China - the greatest B-movie ever made, but it’s actually good, so it might not qualify lol.
9. Satan's Cheerleaders

Death Bed: The Bed that Eats

Killer Sofa

Demon Child 666

Zombies vs Vampires

Pieces

Spider-Baby

## 3. Movies about loneliness/isolation/emptiness

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Hello everyone, please recommend movies with themes of loneliness/isolation/emptiness. They're the kind that brings comfort to me. Something that actually represents those feelings. It doesn't have to be the miserable type of loneliness/isolation/emptiness (though it's okay), and hopefully not romance. Thank you!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lost in Translation
2. Lars and the real girl 
3. Castaway
4. Taxi driver?
5. The Wrestler by Darren Aronosfsky starring Mickey Rourke. 

Absolute Depression. Wouldn't recommend it if you are going through a difficult time or are suffering from any mental health issues.
6. All of Us Strangers
7. Her

The man who sleeps
8. The Station Agent
9. Perfect Days
10. A Single Man 

## 4. Looking for More Chaotic, Hilarious Comedy Movies Like Superbad and Jump Street

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I’ve already watched and loved movies like *Superbad*, *21 Jump Street*, *22 Jump Street*, *Neighbors*, etc. Looking for more genuinely hilarious comedy movies with the same chaotic, raunchy, buddy-comedy vibe.  
Any hidden gems or must-watch recommendations? Open to both older and newer movies.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hot Tub Time Machine 👍 
2. Hot Rod is hilarious 
3. Game Night is great
4. Role Models

But only if you want to rock and roll all day and part of every night.
5. Animal House (1978)
6. Booksmart and Bottoms fit the bill
7. The Other Guys
8. Bridesmaids, Popstar Never Stop Never Stopping
9. Spy! 
10. Hot Fuzz

## 5. Romantic Comedy please

**Score:** 16
**Body:** It's Friday night and my wife and I are looking for something to watch together. We like a good laugh. She'll watch any old rom com but I enjoy something with just a little bit more to it.

We **loved** Nobody Wants This - I know that's a TV show, but this kind of thing in a film would be great.

Silver Linings Playbook is another good example of the kind of thing we enjoy too.

Nothing overly sexy or on the nose sexually (tasteful is fine).

  
What are your favourite Rom Coms and why?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Always Be My Maybe—it addresses grief in a beautiful way and I love movies that feature food.

Long Shot—it addresses politics in a fun way, makes me actually laugh out loud, and I adore the cast.

While You Were Sleeping—just so classic and sweet without being cloying.
2. I'm not a rom com person at all but very much enjoyed Palm Springs from a few years ago. Think it's on Amazon Prime 
3. The Family Man (2000) became an instant classic for me this year. Laughs, tears, Nic Cage! All that good stuff

50/50 (2011)

Garden State (2004)
4. Romancing the Stone -- great comedy with a cute romance
5. Moonstruck 
6. I'm not big into romcoms, but I do like Crazy Stupid Love. It's not perfect but the casting is great and the dynamic between Steve Carell and Ryan Gosling is fantastic. 
7. So I Married an Ax Murder or Romancing the Stone or Coming to America are my go to RomComs for non RomCom people. 
They are solidly comedy and solidly romance. Good acting in all of them and has a plot line that is entertaining. 
8. I Want You Back with Charlie Day and Jenny Slate! The two usually -sidekick/character actors star and are adorable. Formulaic but very well done.

(Confess I partly loved it because I'm much more of a sidekick type myself, have always felt kindred to Slate, and have long thought Day adorable.)
9. Try The Philadelphia Story and /or Bringing Up Baby
10. The princess bride!

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What's the absolute most disturbing transformation scene you've ever watched? I just watched Tusk and I'm honestly speechless

**Score:** 2514
**Body:** The sheer body horror of his transition into the walrus is unmatched. What other films have a transformation that completely shocked you?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The donkey transformation in Pinocchio is still terrifying

Kid screams for his mom, possibly in immense pain, before his screams turn into donkey braying.
  - Traumatized me as a small child. Can't believe that was a movie for kids lol
    - There's a lot of lowkey disturbing scenes in older Disney movies, i.e. Dumbo's weird drug trip, everything with the Witch in Snow White
2. The Fly 1986
  - ![gif](giphy|fcMMH3hxQmnio)
3. ![gif](giphy|11y6gHEf8QVg6k)  
Witches 
4. District 9 is pretty rough. 
5. The army turning into pigs in Willow is a core memory for me. "You are all Pigs."
6. Color Out Of Space where the mum & son end up fused together.
7. The shunting scene in “Society”  


https://preview.redd.it/mw93d61hhpdh1.jpeg?width=700&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=d82dd1457ad52bb78d112f68178aef3df2b28a21

## 2. Brenda Fricker has sadly passed away at the age of 81. She played the Pigeon Lady in Home Alone 2: Lost in New York.

**Score:** 1990

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. And she won an Oscar for her supporting  role in My Left Foot. Brilliant performance. 
2. She's also in Angels in the Outfield. RIP
  - Maggie was the best. Such a warm and loving character 
3. She was very funny in So I Married an Axe Murderer. RIP to a talented actress. 
4. Sad news indeed. RIP Nurse Megan
5. First Irish actress to win an Academy Award.

R.I.P. Brenda.
6. I mean i have been praying that a specific actor from home alone 2 would pass but not her, never her. 
  - The cameo?
    - ![gif](giphy|wrBURfbZmqqXu)
  - ![gif](giphy|dxbPGFv7tSK3e)

## 3. ‘The Odyssey’ Backlash Failed Tremendously

**Score:** 975
**Body:** All the trolls in all the world couldn't stop this masterpiece. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There's still a lot of people out there who are largely oblivious to online controversies. Mainstream is still a thing, and Nolan is mainstream. 
2. As a long time user of reddit, i'm happy that reddit is largely irrelevant. 
  - It wasn't just reddit. It was all of social media and most of the hateful comments were bots with little to no comment history. TikTok Odyssey "Go woke, Go broke" hate comments were everywhere.
    - "Helen of Detroit" was the one I saw all over TikTok the last month.

No profile pic and randomised names with no responses to come backs. Just bot work.
      - It's fascinating how anyone could presume to know how a creature spawned from an egg created by Zeus raping Leda while being disguised as a swan would look like. 
3. The best part is that, with how often Nolan is talked about on Reddit, The Odyssey is going to keep showing up everywhere on any movie sub. It’s never going away. 
  - The film subs on Reddit are still trying to convince us that Oppenheimer was a terrible movie, so yeah, they’ll keep bitching about The Odyssey for a few years at least.
4. As a more conservative person of reddit (relative to the masses here), i always felt like the “backlash” was always more of the press magnifying voices of a minority.

I don’t mind any of the casting choices, and feel like I am almost guaranteed at least a good movie with Nolan and Damon, let alone likely a great movie. With that said, im not exactly hyped to see the movie either. I just hope to be entertained for a few hours.
5. Watched it yesterday. It’s fantastic, and the reasons people were mad have about 3 minutes of screen time combined in the 3 hours of movie. 
6. Saw it today, it is fucking amazing, please go watch this in the cinema. 

## 4. My Short Odyssey Review (2026/Nolan/Damon)

**Score:** 577
**Body:** Main Points.

1. Felt like I was watching one long extended trailer. Scenes kept cutting quickly back and forth and nothing ever simmered, causing the movie to feel rushed. Because of that, it was never easy to really get into any of the characters in a deep way or feel drawn to their story.

2. Dialogue audio not good. Hard to hear and understand in many scenes. 

3. If you knew nothing at all about the Odyssey, then a shit load of things that happen in the movie just don't really make sense and aren't fleshed out or explained well. The story, as viewed from going in without knowing anything, was not compelling. It was hard to ever care about anyone.

4. Robert Pattinson turns into a hilarious bad guy at the end: "swords! looks guys there's spears! get the shields!" they kind of make him this ridiculous coward at the end. Kind of boring.

5. The only truly heart tugging scene is the dog.

6. Acting was fine overall. Wish there were more weird characters or strange people. It was max Hollywood. 

7. Charlize, while looking pretty, sort of looks like everyone else now in Hollywood, with the face work, and therefore it drew me out of the scenes cause that whole part with him and her looked like they were in some rich hippie camp in Cancun. Weird.

8. Music was kinda boring. The end credits song was like dark and sad sounding. Just kinda lame overall music and sound, but loud for sure.

9. I wouldn't recommend the movie, but I also wouldn't say don't watch. 

10. If this was the first Christopher Nolan movie I had ever seen, I might be drawn into his unique way of making films and they're editing, but since it's not his first and I've seen this schtick over and over, I kinda think I'm bored with his style of movies.

Conclusion: not terrible, but not a great movie that left me wowed.

Thank you

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Truly one of the movie ever 
  - Well said. Movie ever for sure
2. And why shoot in IMAX when 90% is shot wide open (shallow focus). Hell, why go to all these exotic locations just to not show most of them except with blurred backgrounds. 
3. ![gif](giphy|tJE5tPJBLXcU8)
4. Safe to say that botox ruined hollywood 
  - Nicole Kidman in the Northman really took me out of it 
    - Totally agree. I felt like I was watching a commercial whenever she showed up.
    - Sorry but they need to let these older stars go instead of looksmaxxing them and trotting them into every movie. Hollywood is too afraid to bet on people they don’t already know. 
5. His audio is very badly mixed for most theaters. I didn't know what the hell anyone was saying in Oppenheimer when I went. I actually enjoyed it very much later at home with subtitles. 
  - I didn’t see Oppenheimer (three hours ugh) and part of the reason is because I couldn’t hear a fucking word anyone said in Tenet and I just knew that was going to be the case again for his next movies 

## 5. What’s the most badass line you’ve heard in a movie here’s mine from Beowulf(2007)

**Score:** 417
**Body:** I am Ripper... Tearer... Slasher... Gouger. I am the Teeth in the Darkness, the Talons in the Night. Mine is Strength... and Lust... and Power! I AM BEOWULF!

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Somewhat cliché at this point but:

“I’m not locked in here with you… You’re locked in here with me!”

Rorschach, Watchmen

Edit: Hey my first award. Thanks amigo/a!
  - "The accumulated filth of all their sex and murder will foam up about their waists and all the whores and politicians will look up and shout "Save us!"... and I'll whisper.. "no."'
2. Kingdom of Heaven

"You go to certain death."

"All death is certain."
  - My favorite from koh:

"What is Jerusalem worth?"

Saladin: "Nothing."
(Saladin turns and walks away, then pauses, smiles, and turns back around)

"Everything!"
3. ![gif](giphy|P3ng2C1t8QCJ2)
  - He’s a sexual tyrannosaurus
4. “Luck will often save a man… if his courage holds.”
  - "I cannot lift this"

"Grow stronger"
5. ![gif](giphy|3o752iG3Dqj8QSIlji)

His shift in tone absolutely makes it all the more menacing.
6. “I did not hit her! It’s not true! It’s bullshit! I did not hit her! I did not!” 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Double Indemnity (1944): My God! What a movie. Literally every single thing you want in a flick. The slow, step by step, inexorable closing of the net around the protagonist as he struggles mightily against his inevitable fate is absolutely riveting to watch.

**Score:** 114
**Body:** Spoilers for a 75 year old movie

 instead of writing a long detailed review of one of the most reviewed movies of all time I'll just get some highlights

 - the fact that the cat and mouse game of the movie is played between two people who deeply respect each other and are great friends elevates this movie to the next level

- the slow realization that the woman he's dealing with isn't just a bad person like himself, but she's pure straight evil is at the core of the movie. He realizes he's in over his head and scrabbles trying to figure out how to make things right

- I like the fact that the murder actually went off without a hitch. It was basically the perfect crime, and yet despite being the perfect crime fate had another idea

- pulling up to a drive in restaurant and ordering a beer that sits on a tray balanced on the driver side door, drinking your beer and then driving off, is really quite something isn't it?

- the lead female character is bold, strong, intelligent, conniving, sexy etcetera. Obviously she's a bad person to put it lightly. But she's the major plot mover

- the protagonist hanging out with his own murder victims daughter and becoming her trusted confidante is so weird and twisted, it was just diabolical. And yet there was something pure about it because he genuinely felt terrible about what he'd done and sort of wanted to make it right

- if you put on dark sunglasses and talk to a stranger in whispered tones in the middle of a grocery aisle that's a completely normal thing to do. Not suspicious at all! 😂

- this movie perfects what I call “situational high tension”, where the scene is just a few people in A room and yet the relation of those people and the secrets they hold creates extraordinary dramatic tension

- overall if you haven't seen this movie you absolutely owe it to yourself to see it. Just get over the fact that it's black and white. You will forget about the fact you don't like black and white movies in about 5 minutes into this movie.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Unpopular opinion: of course this is an almost perfect movie (and I love it), but I dare to say that is one of those (relatively few) legendary movies where the Hays Code actually limits the work. 

Of course I don't mean that the movie should be more 'explicit' or visually adult, I mean that if it could be A LITTLE more suggestive and sensual (even just on the level of "It Happened one Night" would be enough) the movie would actually be even better, 'cause the strength of the sudden passion would be more believable. 

Still one of the greatest of course. 
  - >Unpopular opinion: of course this is an almost perfect movie (and I love it), but I dare to say that is one of those (relatively few) legendary movies where the Hays Code actually limits the work.

I concur. You don't need a full sex scene but turning the sexiness of one or two scenes by 10%-20% would really elevate the tension in those scenes. 

That said [this scene is perfect.](https://www.reddit.com/r/movies/comments/1nh0xzd/double_indemnity_1944_accident_insurance_scene/)
  - At one point they're lounging around on the couch at his apartment and it's pretty obvious that they were supposed to have just had sex... I think?
    - That's the only interpretation that makes sense.  First they're kissing very passionately.  Then he's reclining on the couch very languorously smoking a cigarette and she's reapplying her makeup.  Yep, they did it.
    - yeah I mean there are plenty of Code movies where you can argue that the Code help them remain timeless and iconic and whatever... but I really think if this was made with just a little more freedom, you could argue for it being the greatest movie of all time and no one could say that it's hyperbolic. The anklet is genius touch but... it's really not enough to sell it completely imho.
      - Love the sexual banter though

Walter Neff: You'll be here too?

Phyllis: I guess so, I usually am.

Walter Neff: Same chair, same perfume, same anklet?

Phyllis: I wonder if I know what you mean.

Walter Neff: I wonder if you wonder.
        - That's actually more titillating and evocative than most 'sexy' lines. 

As usual, constraints foster creativity ;)
  - The film adaptation of The Big Sleep was destroyed by censorship too. If you haven't read the book it can be quite difficult to figure out what is going on. 
    - Yeah that movie is confusing as hell, but it did help give us the big lebowski so there's that
2. Yeah, I think it's the best movie of 1944, easily. Also no. 14 (out of 250) on my personal ranking of the best movies from the 40s. And also, it's probably in my top 10-15 noir films of all time.

I think my favorite moment is when Stanwyck confesses her love, right before MacMurray shoots her. 

Funnily enough I actually hated Indemnity when I was a teenager, after seeing it for the first time. Took me more than 10 years to revisit it and realize what a fool I've been.

## 2. Should Nolan Always Use IMAX? On The Odyssey, Immersion and Editing Issues

**Score:** 78
**Body:** Let me preface this by saying that the Odyssey does what Nolan's been doing in his last few movies - put a bombastic spectacle on the big screen. But, like Oppenheimer, I am not sure it is best enjoyed on the biggest screens. Let me explain why I think that.

**Immersion**  
  
In my opinion, IMAX on a proper massive IMAX screen works best for films that give you large vistas to explore, that linger on a frame so you can take it all in. A feast for the eyes, but also an invitation to dive in and immerse yourself into the film.

Avatar The Way of Water, for all its narrative faults (even more so in the third film), does this brilliantly. It's very long, but lots of its runtime is used to show you its vibrant, beautiful world - and it makes you care that the humans come to destroy it, and makes you care that it needs to be protected. Just by letting me be immersed, you made me care. IMAX adds to that emotional impact.

Dune (both 1 and 2) work a treat in IMAX too. The desert landscapes of Arrakis are given time to breathe, you get a real feel for the place you're in, at times it almost feels like a documentary of this foreign place. It's beautifully shot, and again, really lets you explore the space you're in.

Enter Nolan. He, too, makes bombastic, big budget films. Perfectly suited to give us that breathing room and take it all in. But he never gives the viewer the space to do this. This happened in Oppenheimer, and it is happening here. We just don't get those grand vistas of the places we're in. I thought that in Odyssey, we might get that. Perhaps a shot of the sea where we see the tiny ship they're on, to give us some perspective there. Or how small they are compared to the horrors they encounter. But those shots simply don't exist. Oppenheimer could have had this impact as well with the atom bomb test, yet both the explosion and the way it was delivered was underwhelming, in what was supposed to be the highlight of the film. Oppenheimer felt claustrophic, like the walls closing in on Oppenheimer, as he barreled towards the inevitable outcome he felt he needed to push towards - and IMAX just feels like a forced format to push that story into, and it doesn't work I think.  
He HAS done it right before. Look at Dunkirk. Those dog fight scenes on film are still some of my favourite scenes. They feel fantastic, it's a well-paced film, and does what it aims to achieve perfectly. It might be my favourite of his films. We get many wider shots of the beach, we get bits of nothingness that lets us take it in and linger, it all works better.  


**The Editing**  
  
Another issue with Nolan, and it really bothered me in Odyssey, is the way he cuts dialogue scenes. I wonder again whether this comes down to how hard it is lugging around a 400 pound camera, or cutting actual film to edit these scenes, but scenes that feel like they deserve to breathe a bit get cuts every 2 seconds for no reason - why? can we not stick with a character until they finish their sentence? 

Slight spoiler ahead.

Especially the scene where Telemachus arrives in Sparta, and he is invited to sit with Menelaus. We go from Telemachus to Menelaus to his Mentor to a side shot to now the Mentor standing next to the table, who is then denied to sit down, to Telemachus sitting down, and all the while they're having a conversation. Before we even know what happened the scene ends and we're in the next one. If it is high stakes, we're in a hurry, fine. But these guys literally just sat down to chat and eat. We see the environment, but always from the eyes of the character. Never just some unimportant characters doing things to set the scene. I have no idea what Sparta looks like other than an abstract backdrop for rushed conversations.  
  
This happens all over. No establishing shots. No wides of both characters on screen talking. Nothing but mediums to close ups of the characters in the scene. It all feels rushed and cut without a thought. And it's the cheapest thing to get right, the easiest thing to not mess up. I don't understand then that this is the deliberate choice. Are we seriously rushing a movie that's already almost 3 hours in places where slowing down is most needed? Does anybody actually enjoy this sort of editing?

And again, this sort of editing is poison for IMAX. I barely get a chance to register what's going on on the almost 500 square meters of screen in front of me. To me this kind of cutting is jarring regardless of screen size, but it's especially brutal on a screen that big. It's like Nolan doesn't want to allow me to get immersed. It's like he hates that he needs dialogue and would rather cut those scenes entirely, but feels like they're necessary to not have a plot of loose ends. But I'd rather have voiceover exposition with pretty vistas of the places than this.



I think both of these issues make me feel wanting after watching Nolan's films on the big screen. Curious to hear what you all think of this.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think this is some fair criticism, it's certainly not to my own taste.

Nolan is a montage theorist, but that can manifest different ways in different montage theorists.

For the most part, I have conceded that I like the 'Nolan experience' and his approach is cutty. I would argue he does have the shots you want, but he never holds onto them the way I'd like.

I think this became most obvious to me starting with Dunkirk just because he marketed the film with this extraordinary shot where he held on the mole as all the soldiers take cover as the sound of the bomb dropping builds.

In the actual film, he cuts to an aerial shot that dissipates the pictorial effect of that shot, but adds another effect, juxtaposing their experience with the POV of the bomber, showing just how they're fish in a barrel.

But I'm sympathetic to your overall criticism, even if I have accepted it for the most part as key to the Nolan effect (things I accept less would include the hokeyness of some of his dialogue). It can sometimes feel inelegant, and I think your citing the scene at Sparta is a good shout.

But it's worth noting in terms of the IMAX of it all. This recalls a criticism Spielberg had of the format decades ago, he said you couldn't apply montage theory to it because the screen was too large for the eye to travel. This is notable because Spielberg is far more likely to hold a shot than Nolan.

But I think it works for Nolan for one key reason. A couple people here are ignoring the actual text of your post and complaining that the film is not released in 1.43:1 in standard theaters. But the fact of the matter is he composes for 2.39:1. That is the truest ratio. Everything else is keeping the frame safe for other exhibition formats.

If you watch it in 15/70 as I did yesterday, you don't notice any of the compositions, or the frame, you're just in it, which is really cool, but all the compositions are kind of indifferently wider than they need to be. 

Because he frames everything in 2.39:1, everything tends to get pulled into the center of the composition and your eyes don't need to travel too much. 

I think you're still in the right to dislike it, but I think that is part of the reason it more or less works for so many.
2. I think that's a fair criticism. It bothered me quite a lot on Interstellar too. Barring a few scenes, everything else is shot so tight I started to feel frustrated. I hated the shots where the camera stuck to the craft and vehicles and gave us such a narrow pov. The water planet for example could've been so much more. We never see a wide shot of what it looks like from space for example. How can you not put that in the film? 
  - lol the cameras stuck to the spacecraft are quite literally my favorite scenes from interstellar, to each their own I guess 
3. Regarding the context of this post, I'm convinced that Nolan is a full "find it in the edit" director. He's gone on record as not using shot lists and deciding how to shoot on set, a looser improvisational method that breeds collaboration. The final product of all of his films always feels like one giant montage - info is delivered fast and furious with little time to sit with it, scenes are short and to the point, in a way that feels like it would be wildly inefficient to shoot if scripted this way, which leads me to conclude that Nolan's movies tend to be cut down in the edit and are longer, fuller scenes on the day of shooting. I think this is the secret sauce to his popularity - his movies have a rapid pace and info is thrown at the audience constantly and the scenes are all glued together with bombastic music and Foley so you end up feeling it more than reading it.

I am concerned though that the decision to shoot The Odyssey entirely in IMAX will be a detriment to the final product. Up to this point I have felt that the strongest aspect of his IMAX photography has been the way it can be used to emphasize an edit and create feeling. The hard cuts from widescreen to full screen IMAX have a staggering impact on the viewer as kind of an exclamation point. I think back to his first IMAX foray, The Dark Knight, beginning calmly with studio vanity plates and those blue flames revealing the logo at the center, and right as the darkness takes hold we smash to a wide of downtown Chicago on IMAX 70mm, blowing us out of our seats and into the city. Even at home on Blu-ray this edit is highly effective, especially on 4K UHD where the difference in the image quality is noticeable - the clarity and color especially. How will the IMAX photography play over 3 hours and will Nolan still be able to emphasize moments in the edit without this tool in his roster? I won't know until around 3am Sunday.
4. I'd be fine with the whole 70mm fetishism if they actually released IMAX framed versions to digital. There are what like 40 movie theaters in the world that can show this movie "how it's ment to be seen," for the vast majority of people they literally can not see it, and those that do happen to live within a few hours of one are fighting for tickets that sold out months ago. ## 3. What's the point of "x movie is overrated" posts?

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Obviously there's nothing wrong about not liking something that is beloved by a lot of people. It's basic critical thinking and it's good to share your opinion (specially on this sub when the idea is to have a conversation)

I have my own "hot takes", and probably everyone here has their own. I would even say that labeling everyone who doesn't like a beloved movie a contrarian just because their experiences is unfair.

Once that said, I have never understood the point of not saying why you didn't like a movie, but to prove that it is "overrated"

The idea of something been overrated is kinda funny to me because it open the questions "What makes a movie overrated in first place?" And specially, What's the difference between finding a well liked movie bad and that the movie is overrated?" 

When someone calls anything overrated it to me feels less about discussing the quality of a film and more to prove that the people who enjoyed it are "in the wrong" for liking that movie **just** because it doesn't match with YOUR opinion.

So what's the point? Proving that everyone's dumb except you? Or making people open their eyes and see "the truth"?  Why you from all people would have the truth if something is or isn't overrated?

But yeah.

Like I said, i think that negative opinions or critiques are necessary and should be encouraged, but the "overrated" conversations are not adding anything to the discussion, at least to me.

Would love to hear everyones opinion 




### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Like many things on Reddit, people find comfort in being validated by others sharing their opinion.  If you can find a group of people that agree with you that some critically loved movie is "overrated", you don't have to worry that you have bad taste, you can just assume that you and your fellow Redditors are wiser than everyone else.

Like you say, we all have opinions that are unpopular, and that is fine. What would really be nice is if everyone would appreciate movies based on their own personal tastes and not worry about whether others agree or not. But that's not really how we are wired as humans.
  - This is not unique to reddit. This is just people in general. 
2. With you on this.  I usually don't respond.

Those posts get tons of engagement, while it's extremely rare that anyone gains any new perspective, or even that there's room for critiques that go deeper than calling broad swathes of the movie like acting or cinematography globally bad, or one of my least favorites, "unbelievable" (it's not wrong to say that something violated suspension of disbelief or took you out of an experience you were otherwise enjoying, but even just at that next layer down, there's already a lot more to talk about).  

I'm sure sometimes these threads are being posted in something approaching good faith, and it's just that the person posting hasn't immersed themselves in critical vocabulary or concepts.  If that's the case I've seen people around here to generally be pretty helpful with talking through how they broadened their tastes and deepened their experiences.  People are always happy to recommend books, youtubers, or other movies to watch that give a wider basis for talking about the one being critiqued.  In rarer but I think more interesting cases, sometimes you get people reflecting on who they are and what life experiences they brought to the movie, which offers some real chance for human connection with what they're writing.  Any of those would be avenues to something more interesting and alive than "overrated".  

Anyone else that's making "overrated" posts out of looking for an argument or trying to drag down other peoples' experiences of something they liked or whatever, those people can have it and maybe the rest of us can just move on to some more fruitful discussion.
3. People can't accept that they can just *not* like something, because then they're not included in the big online thing so they resort to saying "no, everyone is just *pretending to like it* because how else could most people like it, but not me?"

Then they follow with what I call the "Bad Trifecta" to explain why it's overrated: bad acting, bad dialogue/script, bad cinematography. Every single time; none get any more details, just all bad. Bonus points if they list plotholes that aren't plotholes.

I've stopped interacting with any "review" calling a movie overrated because 99.9% of the time it's just bad faith, forced contrarianism.
4. I've always loathed the term "overrated." If you have your own thoughtful reason why you don't like a popular thing, go ahead and share. But like that's just your opinion. Even when I personally don't like something that's pretty beloved (Poor Things, Rogue One, Jodorowsky's Dune, Avengers Endgame, for a few examples of varying popularity), I still try to leave room for the fact that a lot of people did connect with it in a valid and sincere way.

If I'm saying that it's overrated, then it feels like that's just me flatly declaring that my reasons for disliking it are more valid and intellectual than everyone else's reasons for liking it. And that's a very incurious and self-important way of thinking IMO. Just think what you think and let your reasoning speak for itself instead of adding these meaningless qualifiers to it.

Tl;dr: I agree

5. Agree completely. The other thing I find a little irritatingly funny is people on the one hand firmly believing that opinions are completely objective and there’s no such thing as good or bad art, but then in their next breath arguing vehemently why something is good or bad. 

## 4. Thoughts on The Odyssey (2026)

**Score:** 14
**Body:** I think *The Odyssey* is Nolan’s best film. By this I do not mean only that it is the film of his I enjoyed most, or that it is his largest film, or even that it is technically his most accomplished. All of those things may be true, but they are not what made me think of it as his best. What I felt while watching it was that the films Nolan had made over the previous ten or fifteen years had been preparing him for this one.

I do not mean that he had planned it, or maybe he did?! These patterns are usually clearer afterwards. A director returns to the same problems, the same structures and the same technical ideas, and eventually a film appears in which these things no longer feel separate. What looked like repetition begins to look like preparation.  
  
The scale of *Interstellar* is here. The treatment of time from *Dunkirk* and *Tenet* is here. The moral seriousness of *Oppenheimer* is here. Nolan’s increasing interest in sound as something physical rather than merely musical is here too. In some of the earlier films, these qualities seemed to compete with one another. The scale could become empty. The structures could become mechanical. The sound could seem like a way of forcing importance upon an image that did not possess enough importance by itself. In *The Odyssey*, these things belong together.  
  
The cave sequence is the clearest example. The sound is so loud and so strange that after a while I stopped trying to understand it. I was not thinking about what it represented. I was not thinking about how Nolan and his sound team had produced it. I was not even thinking about whether it was good sound design. I was simply inside it. I don’t think I heard sound like that in a cinema before. And I think this is an unusual achievement for Nolan because he is a director who usually wants things to be understood. He explains the rules of dreams, time travel, memory, physics, history and politics. Sometimes the explanations are necessary, but sometimes his need to explain weakens what the images have already made clear. The cave sequence does the opposite. There is nothing to solve. The sound does not tell us what to think. It does not even give us enough distance to think about it. It becomes the experience itself. I cannot remember another moment in a Nolan film where his technical power was used so completely without becoming an argument for its own importance. The sequence does not say, look at the scale of this film. It does not say, listen to how overwhelming this is. It simply overwhelms. There is another sequence of Odysseus and his crew riding out a storm, and it was outstanding watching it in IMAX.

That is why I think of the film as a culmination. In my opinion, Nolan has always wanted to make films that operate through ideas and through physical sensation at the same time. Often the two sides have remained visible. You could feel the construction underneath the experience. Here, at his best, the construction disappears.  
  
But the film also carries two problems from *Oppenheimer*. I do not remember either problem being as obvious in Nolan’s earlier films. His films had other weaknesses. They could be cold, excessively expository, and too pleased with the neatness of their structures. But these two problems seem newer.  
  
The first is the unevenness of the narrative. This is not the same as saying that the narrative is nonlinear. A nonlinear narrative can be perfectly controlled. The story can arrive in fragments, move backwards and forwards, and withhold essential information, while still giving the sense that every piece is in its proper place. The viewer may not know the whole design yet, but the film knows it. Nolan has done this well before. *Memento* is the most obvious example. Its structure is complicated, but the complication is the film. The form does not interrupt the experience of the story. It produces the experience.

An uneven narrative is different. The film moves through its material in such a way that certain scenes or sections seem to receive the wrong amount of attention. An important event may pass quickly. A less important one may be extended. A transition that should feel inevitable instead requires the viewer to perform some work, not because the film is constructively challenging us, but because the dramatic movement has not been properly shaped.

That was one of my problems with *Oppenheimer*. The narrative structure was clearly deliberate. Nolan did not accidentally make the film that way. But the fact that something is deliberate does not make it successful. There were moments when the structure seemed to complicate material that did not need to be complicated. The viewer had to assemble the pieces, but assembling them did not always produce a deeper understanding of the characters or the events.

The complexity became work.  
  
*The Odyssey* sometimes has the same problem. It meanders, but not with complete confidence. There is a difference between a story that appears to wander while secretly knowing its destination and a story that makes the viewer responsible for preserving its direction. At times this film feels like the second kind.

The pieces do come together. That is not the issue. The issue is the experience of reaching that point. I sometimes felt that the film had not given enough dramatic weight to the connections it was asking me to make. I could understand what had happened, but understanding it required me to supply something the film had hurried past.  
  
Perhaps this is what Nolan wants. Perhaps he now prefers narrative pressure to narrative clarity. But I am not convinced the material benefits from it. The story already contains distance, absence, disguise, memory and uncertainty. It does not need the structure to create an additional layer of difficulty.  
  
The second problem is more serious because it affects the individual scenes. Nolan does not let them breathe. Again, this became obvious to me in *Oppenheimer*. There is a scene in which Oppenheimer gives his children to a friend because he and his wife are unable to care for them properly.  
  
So Oppenheimer asks another man to take his children. This is an enormous event in the lives of everyone in the room. It contains shame, exhaustion, failure and trust. It tells us something terrible about Oppenheimer’s domestic life and about his ability to remain responsible for ordinary human needs while pursuing the work that consumes him. The scene gives us the event, but it does not give us its effect.  
  
Almost as soon as the decision has been communicated, Nolan cuts away. We do not remain with Oppenheimer. We do not remain with the man who has been asked to take the children. We do not see how either of them absorbs what has just happened. The scene treats the information as its conclusion. Once the audience understands that Oppenheimer is leaving his children there, the scene’s work is considered complete.   
  
But the information is not the whole scene. Often the most important moment comes after the necessary words have been spoken. A character has said something, or been told something, and then must continue to exist in the room. The face changes. The body settles differently into a chair. The person looks away. Sometimes nothing visible happens, but the lack of movement becomes expressive because we understand what is being contained.

This is not wasted time. It is where the event becomes part of the character.  
  
I think of the garden scene in *The Godfather*. Vito Corleone speaks to Michael about the life he had wanted for him. He tells him that he had hoped Michael would become something outside the family, perhaps a senator or a governor. He tells him that there was not enough time. He also warns him that the person who arranges the meeting with Barzini will be the traitor. Here, the dialogue ends. Vito leaves. Everything required for the plot has already been said. Coppola could cut to the next scene. Instead, the camera remains with Michael. He is left alone with what his father has told him. He sinks back slightly. He breathes. We see the weight arriving.  
  
The moment is simple. Nothing is added to the story in the ordinary sense. We already know that Michael has taken control of the family. We already know that he is becoming what his father was. But seeing him sit there makes that knowledge real. The position has passed to him, and for a few seconds we watch him feel it. The pause does not explain the emotion. It allows the emotion to exist.  
  
Nolan has started increasingly removing this part of the scene.  
  
So, there is a similar moment in *The Odyssey* when the son (Holland) reaches the kingdom of Jon Bernthal’s character. The meeting matters because the young man has grown up in the absence of his father. His father has been gone for years. He has come to speak to someone who knew him and who may be able to give him some understanding of who his father was. So, Jon Bernthal tells him to sit. He sits. This sequence abruptly cuts. But the act of sitting is where the scene should open up. These two people are now facing one another. One of them is looking for his father in the memories of the other. The other is looking at the son of a man he once knew. The silence between them must contain curiosity, grief, recognition, and uncertainty. I wanted to remain there for a bit. I mean, I did not need more dialogue. I did not need the characters to explain the significance of the meeting. I only wanted the film to look at them for a little longer. Let Holland sit a bit longer. Let Bernthal study him. Let us wonder whether he sees the father in the son. Ten seconds might have been enough. Twenty seconds would have changed the entire weight of the scene. Instead, Nolan cuts at the moment the dramatic information has been established. The son has arrived. The two men have met. We understand the relationship. The narrative can proceed.

This gives the film momentum, but in my opinion, momentum is not always movement. Sometimes a film moves forward by remaining still. Something changes in the character, and because we have been allowed to see the change, the next scene begins in a different emotional place. When these pauses are removed, the events remain events. They do not always become experiences. I mean, of course Nolan knows this.   
  
So, I do not understand why Nolan has begun editing this way. It must be a choice. His films are long, but he appears increasingly afraid of duration within a scene. He will devote enormous amounts of time to the architecture of a narrative, to parallel action and to the accumulation of historical or mythological detail, but he seems reluctant to give forty seconds to the emotional aftermath of a conversation. Perhaps he thinks these pauses are sentimental. Perhaps he believes the actors have already communicated everything necessary. Perhaps he wants the energy of the film never to fall. But there is a difference between sentimentality and attention. To remain on a face is not to tell the audience what to feel. It is simply to acknowledge that a person needs time to understand what has happened to him.  
  
The thing is Nolan’s earlier films did not always have this anxiety. They could be emotionally distant (at times), but they were sometimes willing to remain inside that distance. Now the cuts often come too soon. The film knows the meaning of the scene and moves on before the character has had time to know it. I think this may also be connected to the unevenness of the narrative. Nolan has become so concerned with the movement of the whole that the individual scenes are denied their proper shape. Each scene must pass its information to the next one. The film accumulates power, but individual moments are not always allowed to complete themselves.  
  
This is frustrating because *The Odyssey* contains images and situations that deserve time. It is a story about long absence, about people waiting for someone who may no longer exist as they remember him, about a son trying to form an idea of a father he barely knows, and about a man returning to a world that has continued without him. So these are not merely narrative facts, right? They are conditions that should be visible in the way people look, wait, and hesitate. We can see the film understands the physical scale of these ideas. It understands the sea, the distance, the danger and the violence. It understands what it means for a journey to feel larger than a man. I am less certain that it always understands the smaller durations through which this scale becomes human.  
  
Even so, I think it is Nolan’s best film. The flaws are real, and they are not minor. They affect the form of the narrative and the emotional life of the scenes. They prevent the film from becoming as complete as it might have been. But its achievements are greater.  
  
I believe the frames have a force Nolan has not reached before. The sound is not simply loud or technically impressive; at its best it creates a state in which analysis temporarily becomes impossible. World-class sound design. The world here has weight. The bodies, landscapes and structures feel subjected to the same physical reality. Nolan’s usual division between the intellectual and the sensory is less visible here than in any of his previous films.  
  
At its greatest moments, the film does not ask to be admired. And it does not explain why it matters! It simply exists with such a force that the question disappears. That is what happened in the cave. Sadly, it is also why the scenes that end too early bother me so much.  
  
I believe Nolan has reached a point where he can create complete physical immersion, like perfected it to its maximum level now, but he does not always trust the stillness of a human face. He will allow sound to remain until it ceases to mean anything and becomes pure sensation, but he will not always allow silence to remain until it becomes emotion. This film contains both tendencies. One is the culmination of his career. And the other, I’m afraid, may be the limitation of the films he makes next.

4. *The Odyssey* is a very solid film, and I liked it considerably more than *Oppenheimer*, which suffered from a lack of urgency or organization (it was too much a real biography, a jumble of events that happened to one man, one after another).  Here there is a very clear end that all the wanderings are aiming for, and Nolan effectively ratchets up the tension much as Homer did, like a tightening bowstring.

To my mind, what was most interesting were Nolan’s deviations from the source material, the most far-reaching one being to make the gods only “apparent,” as the first words on screen prominently state.  Homer tells a tale where Poseidon, Zeus, and especially Athena actively intervene, making Odysseus beautiful or disguising him as the plot needs.  Despite the presence of a giant cyclops, a witch, a giant man-eating monster, and an underworld teeming with the dead, Nolan otherwise denudes his odyssey of overt divine intervention. 

This is a very 21st century vision despite the monstrous eruptions: Odysseus’ critical self-reflection is the ultimate payoff, a substantial alteration the archaic Greeks would have been baffled by.  Likewise the self-aware invocations of “civilization” and “peoples from the sea” are very much a contemporary analysis and not at all Homeric.  This all leads to the denouement, >!which is a very Nolanesque ride off into the sunset, reminiscent of his last Batman (and again featuring Anne Hathaway).  That seemed to me not only alien to the subject matter but also a rather forced evasion!<. 

But overall, I liked it plenty.  Some of the fight scenes were annoyingly dark and crowded and as a result tensionless (the cave, the finale), showing Nolan still is not the action director he’s cracked up to be.  And playing Agamemnon as dark blue Vader was over-the-top. Nonetheless, his actors were all excellent (Hathaway in particular dominates her scenes), the editing was quite on point (the sudden cut mid-dialogue to Clytemnestra was spectacular), and the story held the audience spellbound for three hours.  This is what a big Hollywood epic is supposed to deliver. De Mille would be jealous.

## 5. My first video essay got lots of copyright claims, what did I do wrong?

**Score:** 12
**Body:** &#x200B;

**TL;DR**: I uploaded my first YouTube video essay (27 minutes) and tried to be as careful as possible with copyright: no original soundtrack, mostly short muted clips with my own voice-over, a few brief dialogue-only scenes, and some still images. Even so, Content ID claimed roughly 60–70% of the video. Since I regularly see other small creators making similar essays without getting this many claims, I'm wondering what I might have done differently and what I can do to avoid this, so I edit the video again and get able to upload it. You will find more details on how I put the video together in the text.

Also, on what other platforms could I upload the video, even if I lose visibility compared to youtube, just to have it somewhere online and share it with my people?

Thanks a lot!

\---

Hi guys,

After several years writing personal reviews from most of the films I watch, I felt like learning some editing basics and do something like a deeper analysis in a video, with my voice, where I put my reflections, thoughts, etc.

I went with Le Samouraï (1967), the polar classic by Melville because I recently rewatched it and I found out there was not a real essay of that film in my native language, Spanish, being most of the videos kind of summaries.

I took into account from the beginning to be careful with copyright. Didn't use any original soundstrack or music, not even for a second. I did put some scenes where I got the ambient sound, though. Just a few of them where there is absolutely no background sound but the dialogue of some characters. The only sound that is not a dry dialogue is the canary's tweet. 

The video ended up being pretty long, 27 minutes, but I used short clips with no correlation most of the times and stills, so that it doesn't look like I'm somehow recreating the film sequence. Apart from those very few clips that I mentioned due to their relevance and deep weight on what I'm expressing, every clip is completely muted with my voice in off over as a narrator. Nothing different from what we all are used to watch in a video review. Just detailling everything so you can hopefully help me to find the problem. Last thing, I also used some clips from a few films to compared them to Le Samouraï but, again, muted and short clips, referencing them within all the time they appear on screen. With short clips I mean between 2-8 seconds and almost never over 12.

When I uploaded it to my youtube channel as my first video ever, although the account was created about ten years ago and I've always been active, copyrights jumped all over the video. I mean, not just at some parts, but about... 60-70% of its total duration? So, much more than those dangerous parts. I see a lot of videos where short clips with sound or dialogue are used as I did, though. And not really big channels, just people like me or you.

Would appreciate some advice. Also, if you made it til here, thank you, fr. I know I wrote a big one this time. 

Reading y'all!

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dispute it. It will likely get rejected automatically, then appeal it. It will then get dropped and/or expire after 7 days.  

If it's truly fair use and your work is a transformative video essay, you have nothing to worry about. Claims are automatic, so are the immediate rejections to your disputes. But then the appeal forces them to do something about it in a week. They won't, and you win.
2. Try flipping the image. The automated tools image match your video, but tends to get easily fooled by flipping the picture. Also, shrinking the size of the scene you're using and putting it in a frame can trick the system.
